column1: Note
Cooking pasta in heavily salted water helps season the pasta from the inside as it absorbs water while boiling.
Pasta should be stirred during the first few minutes of boiling to prevent sticking together.
Al dente pasta means the pasta is cooked but still firm when bitten.
Adding oil to pasta water is unnecessary and prevents sauce from sticking properly.
Boiling pasta requires a large amount of water to ensure even cooking.
Sautéing garlic too long causes it to burn and develop a bitter taste.
Onions release natural sugars when cooked slowly over low heat.
Caramelization occurs when sugars break down under high heat, creating brown color and rich flavor.
Deglazing a pan involves adding liquid to dissolve browned food bits stuck to the bottom.
A roux is made by cooking flour and fat together and is used to thicken sauces.
Béchamel sauce is a white sauce made from milk thickened with a roux.
Simmering means cooking liquid just below boiling with small bubbles forming gently.
Boiling is a rapid cooking method with vigorous bubbling throughout the liquid.
Blanching involves briefly boiling food and then rapidly cooling it in ice water.
Blanching vegetables helps preserve color and texture before freezing.
Roasting uses dry heat in an oven to cook food evenly from all sides.
Baking refers to cooking food using dry heat in an enclosed space like an oven.
Grilling cooks food using direct high heat from below.
Steaming cooks food using vapor from boiling water without direct contact with liquid.
Steaming preserves more nutrients compared to boiling vegetables.
Searing meat creates a browned crust through high heat and Maillard reaction.
The Maillard reaction is responsible for browning and flavor development in cooked proteins.
Resting meat after cooking allows juices to redistribute evenly.
Marinating helps tenderize meat and adds flavor through acidic or enzymatic action.
Acidic marinades often include lemon juice or vinegar.
Tenderizing meat physically breaks down muscle fibers for softer texture.
Slow cooking allows tough cuts of meat to become tender over long periods.
Pressure cooking uses steam pressure to reduce cooking time significantly.
Frying cooks food in hot oil to create a crispy exterior.
Deep frying fully submerges food in hot oil for even crisping.
Shallow frying uses less oil and cooks food partially submerged.
Baking soda can help brown foods faster due to increased alkalinity.
Salt enhances flavor by suppressing bitterness and enhancing sweetness.
Sugar can balance acidity in tomato-based sauces.
Reducing a sauce means simmering it to evaporate water and intensify flavor.
Stock is made by simmering bones, vegetables, and aromatics in water.
Broth is similar to stock but usually lighter and seasoned.
Soup is a liquid-based dish that can contain solids like vegetables or meat.
Knife skills improve cooking efficiency and safety in the kitchen.
A sharp knife is safer than a dull one because it requires less force.
Chopping means cutting food into irregular pieces.
Dicing means cutting food into small, uniform cubes.
Mincing means cutting food into very fine pieces.
Julienne cut refers to cutting vegetables into thin matchstick shapes.
Baking powder contains both acid and base to create rising in baked goods.
Yeast is a living organism that produces carbon dioxide to make dough rise.
Proofing is allowing dough to rise before baking.
Kneading develops gluten structure in bread dough.
Gluten provides elasticity and structure to bread.
Over-kneading can make bread dense and tough.
Resting dough allows gluten to relax for easier shaping.
Bread browning occurs due to caramelization and Maillard reaction.
Folding ingredients gently helps preserve air in mixtures like whipped cream.
Whipping cream incorporates air to increase volume and lightness.
Egg proteins coagulate when heated, causing thickening and structure formation.
Overcooking eggs causes rubbery texture due to excessive protein tightening.
Scrambled eggs are cooked while continuously stirring to create soft curds.
Poaching eggs involves cooking them gently in simmering water without shell.
Frying eggs allows direct heat contact for crispy edges.
Boiling eggs for too long causes greenish yolk due to sulfur reaction.
Ice baths stop cooking immediately after boiling eggs or vegetables.
Blending creates smooth mixtures by breaking down solids into liquids.
Emulsification combines oil and water into a stable mixture like mayonnaise.
Mayonnaise is an emulsion of oil, egg yolk, and acid like vinegar.
Vinaigrette is a simple dressing made of oil and vinegar.
Whisking incorporates air and mixes ingredients smoothly.
Sifting flour removes lumps and aerates dry ingredients.
Measuring ingredients accurately is important in baking chemistry.
Room temperature ingredients mix more evenly in baking.
Overmixing batter can develop too much gluten and create dense baked goods.
Fold gently when combining wet and dry ingredients in baking.
Chocolate melts smoothly when heated slowly to avoid burning.
Tempering chocolate stabilizes cocoa butter for glossy finish.
Salted butter contains added salt for flavor and preservation.
Unsalted butter is preferred in baking for better control of salt levels.
Creaming butter and sugar incorporates air for light baked texture.
Caramel sauce forms when sugar is heated until it melts and browns.
Burnt sugar becomes bitter and unusable in desserts.
Pastry dough should be kept cold to maintain flaky texture.
Shortening creates flakier pastries due to higher melting point.
Pie crust becomes tough if overworked during mixing.
Fermentation develops flavor in sourdough bread over time.
Sourdough starter contains natural yeast and bacteria cultures.
Rising dough should be kept in warm environment for yeast activity.
Boiling vegetables too long causes nutrient loss and mushy texture.
Steaming vegetables preserves texture better than boiling.
Roasted vegetables develop sweetness through caramelization.
Seasoning should be adjusted gradually during cooking rather than all at once.
Taste testing during cooking ensures balanced flavors.
